web.title: Reps in Reserve (RIR): What You Need to Know - NASM
web.description: This article will go over Reps in Reserve (RIR) and how its the best method to quickly reach your goals by using the right weight and repetitions.
web.markdown:
[Facebook](https://facebook.com/personaltrainers "Facebook")[Instagram](https://instagram.com/nasm_fitness "Instagram")[LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/company/national-academy-of-sports-medicine-nasm-?trk=tyah&trkInfo=tarId%3A1411429173284%2Ctas%3Anational+academy+of+sports%2Cidx%3A1-1-1 "LinkedIn")[Pinterest](https://www.pinterest.com/nasmfitness/ "Pinterest")[Twitter](https://twitter.com/nasm "Twitter")[Youtube](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCjWgUFeyDbeQ3Q_eVCup_7Q "Youtube") [![NASM](https://blog.nasm.org/hubfs/Nasm_December2018/images/logo186x62.png)](https://blog.nasm.org/) - [NASM.org](https://www.nasm.org/) - [Fitness](https://blog.nasm.org/tag/fitness) - [CPT](https://blog.nasm.org/tag/cpt) - [Nutrition](https://blog.nasm.org/tag/nutrition) - [CES](https://blog.nasm.org/tag/ces) - [Sports Performance](https://blog.nasm.org/tag/sports-performance) - [Workout Plans](https://blog.nasm.org/tag/workout-plans) - [Wellness](https://blog.nasm.org/tag/wellness) - [![NASM Blog](https://cdn2.hubspot.net/hubfs/2494739/Nasm_December2018/images/logo-mini.png)](https://blog.nasm.org/) [Workout Plans](https://blog.nasm.org/tag/workout-plans "") [Workouts](https://blog.nasm.org/tag/workouts "") # Reps in Reserve (RIR): What You Need to Know ![Woman doing seated dumbbell shoulder press inside gym](https://blog.nasm.org/hubfs/reps-in-reserve.jpg) ![Kinsey Cave](https://blog.nasm.org/hubfs/Kinsey_Headshot.jpg) [Kinsey Cave](https://blog.nasm.org/author/kinsey-mahaffey "")\| Stay Updated with NASM! Most lifters are familiar with [calculating percentages of their 1 Rep Max](https://www.nasm.org/resources/one-rep-max-calculator) (aka, 1RM, the maximum amount of weight that you can lift for 1 repetition of an exercise) to choose the appropriate weight for an exercise based on their desired intensity for the day. What if you don’t know your 1RM for an exercise, but you still want to make sure you get those #gains? The Reps in Reserve (RIR) method is a quick and relatively easy way to choose the right weight to help you reach your goals. ## What Does Reps in Reserve (RIR) Mean? Reps in Reserve (RIR) is rising in popularity as a method to measure the intensity of a lift by describing how many more repetitions you could perform before technical failure (an inability to perform the lift with good form). It’s best to employ this method after you’re comfortable with resistance training and have been familiarized with the scale. Let’s say you’re doing a set of bench press and you do 10 reps. When you finish, you know that you could have done 2 more reps with good form before failing. Your Reps in Reserve (RIR) is 2 in this case, since you could have performed only 2 more repetitions with good form. Calculating a percentage of your 1 Repetition Maximum (RM) to choose the load you need for your lift works, but the calculated load might be too heavy if you’re feeling less than your best, or it might even be too light on the days that you’re functioning at maximum capacity. Using the RIR measurement will automatically adjust for your readiness to train, allowing you to still train at the desired intensity with good form. Additionally, if you don’t know your 1RM for an exercise, RIR can be particularly helpful in helping you nail down the appropriate weight for an exercise. ## Reps in Reserve Chart: Example Workout RIR can be the most useful in building muscular endurance, muscular development (hypertrophy), strength, or the max strength exercises in the power phase (Helms, et. al., 2016). Here is how RIR can be applied to Phases 2-5 of the OPT Model: | | | | | --- | --- | --- | | **Phase of Training** | **Repetitions** | **Recommended RIR** | | Phase 2 Strength Endurance | 8-12RM | RIR 0-1 | | Phase 3 Muscular Development | 6-12RM | RIR 0-2, Note: Train RIR 0 (to failure) only on the last set of a single-joint exercise
RIR 2-4 is best for multi-joint movements, and will avoid excessive muscle damage | | Phase 4 Maximal Strength | 1-5RM | RIR 1-2, and occasionally, RIR 0 | | Phase 5 Power | 1-5 reps for strength and 8-10 reps for power | RIR 2-3
RIR 4 (intensity cap to ensure adequate explosiveness in the movement) | Let’s apply it to a chest and triceps set in Phase 3 Muscular Development, employing the RIR method so that the intensity increases with each set: | | | | | | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | **Exercise** | **Sets** | **Reps** | **RIR** | **Tempo** | | Barbell Bench Press
Incline DB Chest Press
Triceps Rope Extension | 3 | 8 | Set 1: 4 RIR
      Set 2: 3 RIR
       Set 3: 2 RIR
\*For triceps, 0-1   RIR for the last set | Moderate | [![blog ad](http://www.nasm.org/images/2021/blog-ads/nasm---inline-ad---science-behind-effective-weight-loss-750x300---2.jpg?sfvrsn=7b65e753_2)](https://trainer.nasm.org/the-science-behind-effective-weight-loss?utm_source=blog&utm_medium=referral&utm_campaign=BlogAd&utm_content=BlogAd-weightloss-mini-course) [![podcast ad](https://f.hubspotusercontent30.net/hubfs/2494739/RandomFit_750x300.jpg?sfvrsn=9168f937_2)](https://cms.megaphone.fm/channel/NASM8710158605) There are many ways to apply RIR to reach your goals by simply staying within the above guidelines! You can keep the same RIR each set or manipulate it within the recommended range to vary your intensity level (making it harder or easier as you go). ## Reps in Reserve Vs Training to Failure We know that lifting close to failure is critical for muscular hypertrophy and strength development. Leaving some “reps in the tank” by employing the RIR method will accomplish this task without allowing you to go into failure each set. It’s acceptable to train to failure every so often to reach a max strength goal, but training to failure often can cause changes in resting hormone concentrations and increases in strength may be compromised due to over training (Helms et. al., 2016). ## How to Calculate Reps in Reserve For new lifters, it can take some practice to properly gauge how many reps you have left “in the tank” before your form fails. You might practice a lift with a spotter and near the end, call out how many reps you think you have left, and see if you’re correct. This can help teach you to gauge different intensity levels (4-6 RIR, 2 RIR, 1 RIR, etc.) and to know your personal limits. More experienced lifters will have a better feel for how many additional reps they could do once they finish their set, although they could use the same method to test their accuracy. ## Can You Increase Your One-Rep Max with RIR Training? According to the principle of specificity, the best way to increase your 1RM is to train at very heavy loads at low repetitions (aka to practice “maxing out”). RIR training can be a helpful guide when it comes to selecting the intensity required to achieve this goal. To increase your 1RM, you’ll want to train in Phase 4, Maximal Strength Training. Using the guidelines listed above for recommended RIR usage in Phase 4, you’ll want to train in the 1-5 repetition range with an RIR of 1-2, and occasionally progressing to RIR 0 as you’re training to increase your one-rep max. Here’s an example training plan for someone who wants to increase their 1RM of a lift over the course of 4 weeks: Week 1: 5x5, 1-2 RIR Week 2: 3x5, 1-2 RIR Week 3 and 4: Use a Pyramid Set (example below) Set 1: 5 reps 2 RIR Set 2: 4 reps 2 RIR Set 3: 3 reps 1-2 RIR Set 4: 2 reps 1 RIR Set 5: 1 rep 0 RIR \*Be sure to allow for 2-4 minutes of rest between each lift for each muscle group! ## Is RIR Better for Hypertrophy or Strength Training? RIR can be equally effective when applied to hypertrophy or strength training. The key is to train according to the rep ranges and intensity levels listed above according to your goals. ## What is The Difference Between RIR and the RPE Scale? The RPE, or [Rate of Perceived Exertion](https://blog.nasm.org/rate-of-perceived-exertion), is a technique used to express how hard you feel like you are working during an exercise on a scale of 1-10, 1 being very easy and 10 being maximum effort. Similar to the RIR, it’s a method used to measure exercise intensity. The difference is that the lifter reports how they feel (RPE) versus the number of repetitions they could still perform (RIR). Although the RPE measure was originally used for cardio workouts, it is also used to help lifters determine their intensity of effort. Here’s a side by side of how the RPE scale and RIR matchup: | | | | --- | --- | | **RPE3** | **RIR** | | 10 - Maximum Effort | 0 Repetitions Remaining | | 9 - Very Hard | 1 Repetitions Remaining | | 8 - Very Hard | 2 Repetitions Remaining | | 7 - High, Vigorous | 3 Repetitions Remaining | | 5-6 - Moderate to Somewhat Hard | 4-6 Repetitions Remaining | | 3-4 - Light to Moderate | - | | 1-2 - Very Light | - | These two methods can be used in tandem to help lifters gauge overall intensity, based upon the preference of the lifter. If you’re ready to ditch the calculator during strength training sessions, give the Reps in Reserve technique a try for a simple way to choose the right weight for your workout based on your goals. ## Reference: Helms, E. R., Cronin, J., Storey, A., & Zourdos, M. C. (2016). Application of the Repetitions in Reserve-Based Rating of Perceived Exertion Scale for Resistance Training. Strength and conditioning journal, 38(4), 42–49. [https://doi.org/10.1519/SSC.0000000000000218](https://doi.org/10.1519/SSC.0000000000000218) ### The Author ![Kinsey Cave](https://blog.nasm.org/hubfs/Kinsey_Headshot.jpg) ### Kinsey Cave Kinsey Mahaffey, MPH, is a Houston-based fitness educator, personal trainer and health coach who developed her commitment to lifelong fitness while playing Division I volleyball. She’s passionate about helping others cultivate a healthy lifestyle and enjoys educating other fitness professionals who share this vision. She’s a [Master Instructor](https://www.nasm.org/about-nasm/master-instructors) and Master Trainer for NASM. You get rewarded ### NASM Referral Program FAQ - **I know someone who’d love NASM and would like to refer them. How do I start?** To start the referral process, please sign up for our referral program [here](https://grow.surf/p4pq69). Once you’ve signed up, you can share your unique referral link via email, social networks, and direct email invite. Please note, in order to take part in our referral program, terms and conditions apply. You may view the terms of service of our referral program [here](https://grow.surf/p4pq69/terms). - **What is a successful referral?** In order for a referral to count, the person you’ve referred must be brand new to NASM when they sign up. - **How do I know how many of my referrals have signed up?** Once you’ve signed up for our referral program, you will be able to see how many referrals have signed up by logging into to our referral program homepage [here](https://grow.surf/p4pq69). - **If one of my referral signs up, when and how do I get my reward?** We manually approve all rewards. Once your reward is approved, you will receive an email from us notifying you with more details. - **My referral forgot to use my unique referral link to sign up. Is there a way I can still receive credit?** Please contact our team to assist you. ### NASM Referral Program Terms of Service - Self-referrals are not allowed. Cheating will get you permanently banned from our referral program. - We reserve the right to disqualify rewards earned through fraudulent, illegal, or abusive methods. - We will only count referrals on links that are automatically tracked and reported by our system. We will not credit a referral if someone says they signed up or someone says they entered a referral code and it was not tracked by our system. - We reserve the right to change the terms of service for our referral program at any time. - You may not use our referral program for any illegal or unauthorized purpose. You must not violate any laws in your jurisdiction. ## Abstract


Lovegrove, S, Hughes, L, Mansfield, S, Read, P, Price, P, and Patterson, SD. Repetitions in reserve is a reliable tool for prescribing resistance training load. J Strength Cond Res 36(10): 2696-2700, 2022-This study investigated the reliability of repetitions in reserve (RIR) as a method for prescribing resistance training load for the deadlift and bench press exercises. Fifteen novice trained men (age: 17.3 ± 0.9 years, height: 176.0 ± 8.8 cm, body mass: 71.3 ± 10.7 kg) were assessed for 1 repetition maximum (1RM) for deadlift (118.1 ± 27.3 kg) and bench press (58.2 ± 18.6 kg). Subsequently, they completed 3 identical sessions (one familiarization session and 2 testing sessions) comprising sets of 3, 5, and 8 repetitions. For each repetition scheme, the load was progressively increased in successive sets until subjects felt they reached 1-RIR at the end of the set. Test-retest reliability of load prescription between the 2 testing sessions was determined using intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) and coefficient of variation (CV). A 2-way analysis of variance with repeated measures was used for each exercise to assess differences in the load corresponding to 1-RIR within each repetition scheme. All test-retest comparisons demonstrated a high level of reliability (deadlift: ICC = 0.95-0.99, CV = 2.7-5.7% and bench press: ICC = 0.97-0.99, CV = 3.8-6.2%). Although there were no differences between time points, there was a difference for load corresponding to 1-RIR across the 3 repetition schemes (deadlift: 88.2, 84.3, and 79.2% 1RM; bench press: 93.0, 87.3, and 79.6% 1RM for the 3-, 5-, and 8-repetition sets, respectively). These results suggest that RIR is a reliable tool for load prescription in a young novice population. Furthermore, the between-repetition scheme differences highlight that practitioners can effectively manipulate load and volume (repetitions in a set) throughout a training program to target specific resistance training adaptations.

![2025_0908_TAILORED-7](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201024%20961'%3E%3C/svg%3E)

# RIR (Reps in Reserve) Explained: Smarter Training for Muscle Growth & Strength

October 2, 2025

Written by Cody McBroom

3 Min Read

## Key takeaways

### 1

In this article, Cody McBroom of Tailored Coaching Method breaks down what RIR is, why it’s one of the most effective tools for gauging training intensity, and how you can apply it to build more muscle, strength, and endurance.

### 2

RIR stands for Reps in Reserve. It’s a simple yet powerful way to measure training intensity by estimating how many more reps you could perform in a set before reaching failure.

### 3

Using RIR lets you push hard enough for growth without constantly training to failure, which can increase injury risk and hinder recovery.

# **Understanding RIR (Reps in Reserve):**

## **The Key to Smarter Training and Better Results**

When it comes to strength training, most people know that intensity matters. But how do you actually measure how hard you’re training without overdoing it—or leaving gains on the table? That’s where RIR, or _“Reps in Reserve,”_ comes in. In this article, we’re going to break down what RIR is, why it’s one of the most effective tools for gauging training intensity, and how you can apply it to build more muscle, strength, and endurance.

#### You can also watch an overview of RIR, here:

RPE vs RIR: The Smartest Way to Train for Muscle Growth - YouTube

Tap to unmute

[RPE vs RIR: The Smartest Way to Train for Muscle Growth](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rl5QPxLxvGo) [Cody McBroom](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCVh-wwJuR48xSLs9BlX0I1g)

![thumbnail-image](https://yt3.ggpht.com/x8lpQ6AZPpU23aLJqHan_GxJhji9CLG29Xr6Pq59EUcf1kiOmrG7DeYDMct06aYQIMbYl4k3Ng=s68-c-k-c0x00ffffff-no-rj)

Cody McBroom5.93K subscribers

[Watch on](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rl5QPxLxvGo)

# **What is RIR in Weight Training?**

RIR stands for **Reps in Reserve**. It’s a simple yet powerful way to measure training intensity by estimating how many more reps you could perform in a set before reaching failure.

**For example:** if you finish a set of squats and feel like you could have done 2 more reps before failing, that’s an **RIR of 2**.

Here’s a quick breakdown of the scale:

- **RIR 0** – You trained to absolute failure; no reps left.

- **RIR 1–2** – Very close to failure; you could only grind out 1–2 more.

- **RIR 3–4** – Moderate effort; a few reps left in the tank.

- **RIR 5+** – Light effort; plenty of reps left before failure.


👉 Using RIR lets you **[push hard enough for growth](https://tailoredcoachingmethod.com/5-ways-maximize-muscle-growth/) without constantly training to failure**, which can increase injury risk and hinder recovery.

# **Why RIR Matters for Muscle Growth and Strength**

Many lifters either go too hard—training to failure every set—or too easy, leaving progress behind. **RIR provides the middle ground.**

### **Benefits of Training with RIR**

- **Maximizes hypertrophy** – Studies show you can build muscle effectively within 1–3 RIR without always hitting failure (source).

- **Protects recovery** – Failure training is taxing on the nervous system. RIR ensures you can train consistently without burning out.

- **Improves longevity** – By auto-regulating effort, RIR keeps your training sustainable across months and years.


![Cody McBroom, expert trainer out of Seattle, WA and owner of Tailored Coaching method, showing an example of training with a low RIR (high effort) to maximize hypertrophy](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20850%20566'%3E%3C/svg%3E)Understanding how to gauge your effort properly is a key factor in maximizing muscle hyptrophy.

# **How to Use RIR in Your Training**

### **Match RIR to Your Training Goals**

- **Strength** – Stick to lower RIR (1–2) on heavy compound lifts like squats, bench press, and deadlifts.

- **Hypertrophy (muscle growth)** – Aim for 1–3 RIR on accessory lifts to maximize stimulus while maintaining form.

- **Endurance or technique work** – Train at 3–5 RIR to build work capacity and refine movement patterns.


### **Apply RIR Across Training Phases**

- **Accumulation phase** – Use 3–5 RIR to build a base and perfect form.

- **Progressive phase** – Ramp intensity with 2–3 RIR.

- **Peaking phase** – Occasionally push to 0–1 RIR to test strength and peak performance.


### **RIR vs RPE: What’s the Difference?**

RIR is often paired with **[RPE](https://tailoredcoachingmethod.com/rate-of-perceived-exertion-rpe-vs-percentage-based-training-pbt/) (Rate of Perceived Exertion)**.

- **RIR 2 = RPE 8** (You had 2 reps left, so the set felt like 8/10 difficulty).

Using both methods together provides a more accurate measure of effort.


# **Common Mistakes When Using RIR**

- **Underestimating reps left** – Beginners often think they’re closer to failure than they really are.

- **Always training at 5+ RIR** – Leaving too much in the tank prevents growth.

- **Never training to failure** – Occasionally pushing to RIR 0 helps you recalibrate your effort.


# **Practical Tips for Getting Started with RIR**

1. **Track your RIR in a logbook** – Note effort levels to spot trends over time.

2. **Test true failure occasionally** – On safe lifts (machines, isolation work), go to RIR 0 to gauge accuracy.

3. **Auto-regulate intensity** – On low-energy days, stay at 3–4 RIR; on good days, push closer to 1–2 RIR.

4. **Pair with progressive overload** – Increase weight, reps, or sets while keeping RIR steady for long-term growth.


# **RIR for Beginners vs Advanced Lifters**

- **Beginners** – Start with 3–4 RIR while learning technique. You’ll still grow because nearly everything is a stimulus at this stage.

- **Intermediate lifters** – Train mostly at 1–3 RIR to balance stimulus with recovery.

- **Advanced athletes** – Use 0–2 RIR strategically during peak phases to maximize performance.


# **RIR vs Training to Failure**

One of the most common questions is whether **training to failure is better than using RIR**.

- **Failure training** can help build mental toughness and provide maximum muscle fiber recruitment.

- **RIR-based training** allows for more sustainable progress, less fatigue, and better long-term adherence.


👉 The best approach? Use failure training sparingly, and rely on **1–3 RIR for most sets**.

![Online coach Cody McBroom reading his program from the Tailored Trainer App](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20850%20567'%3E%3C/svg%3E)Following a plan that has a strategic programming method, utilizing the RIR scale, is crucial for achieving top tier results.

# **FAQ: RIR (Reps in Reserve) Training**

1. **What does RIR mean in lifting?** RIR stands for Reps in Reserve, a way of measuring how many reps you had left before failure.
2. **How many RIR should I use for muscle growth?** For hypertrophy, aim for **1–3 RIR** on most sets.
3. **What’s the difference between RIR and RPE?** RIR = reps left in the tank. RPE = how hard the set felt on a 1–10 scale. They complement each other.
4. **Should I always train to failure?** No—failure is useful occasionally, but RIR provides a smarter, more sustainable way to train.
5. **Is RIR good for beginners?** Yes. Beginners should start at higher RIR (3–4) to focus on form and safety.
6. **How do I know if I’m estimating RIR correctly?** Test failure occasionally on safe lifts, and compare your estimate with reality.

# **Final Thoughts: Training Smarter with RIR**

RIR isn’t about working less—it’s about **working smarter**. By balancing intensity, recovery, and progression, you’ll see consistent gains without burnout.

Whether your goal is muscle growth, strength, or endurance, **Reps in Reserve is one of the simplest tools to optimize your training**.

👉 Want to actually apply more of these smart training strategies? Check out what we have to offer at **Tailored Coaching Method,** with our Training App, [The Tailored Trainer,](https://onelink.to/uqsr56) or apply for [**Customized Training and 1-on-1 Coaching**](https://tailoredcoachingmethod.com/online-coaching/). You can also follow **Cody McBroom** on Instagram for more science-based coaching insights.

**OLD-SCHOOL BODYBUILDERS** will tell you that the most surefire way [to build muscle](https://www.menshealth.com/fitness/a64058757/muscle-building-plan/) and feel the effects of your effort is [the pump](https://www.menshealth.com/fitness/a19520337/muscle-pump-builds-size-and-strength/). The swollen sensation that comes when your target muscles are engorged with blood is the highlight of training for many meatheads, so much so that "chasing the pump" is a go-to descriptor for a particular approach to workouts, filled with marathon sets to capture the feeling.

Yes, you _can_ build muscle when you chase the pump—but you're leaving gains on the table when you exhaust yourself in its pursuit. There are limitations to what you can accomplish when you're training with that objective, since you'll likely be using [isolation exercises](https://www.menshealth.com/fitness/a39175582/isolation-exercises/) to target specific muscles rather than [compound movements](https://www.menshealth.com/fitness/a44867943/compound-exercises-list/) and lighter weights to be able to take on a high volume of reps. You won't be able to train as intensely as you need to for maximal muscle growth.

There are more effective ways to build muscle and more efficient methods to go about it. Specifically, an approach often called reps in reserve (RIR).

## How to Use Reps in Reserve to Build Muscle

The approach is relatively simple, according to _MH_ fitness director Ebenezer Samuel, C.S.C.S., who broke it down as a solution to the common mistake of chasing the pump in [his Don't List video](https://www.menshealth.com/fitness/a64039102/muscle-building-workout-mistakes/) for building muscle. Using RIR in your workouts will allow you to train with the intensity needed to create stimulus for muscle growth, and the concept is exactly as the name implies: RIR refers to the amount of reps you could realistically perform (with good form) if you were to keep going after the prescribed reps were completed.

Here's how to apply it: For all of the working sets of your session, challenge yourself to the point that you feel like you would only be able to complete two more reps. You're not [training to failure](https://www.menshealth.com/fitness/a19520889/training-to-failure/) here; that approach can be useful in specific scenarios, but you would burn out too quickly to achieve the type of muscle-building stimulus you're hoping to elicit if you fail each set. If your training plan was to perform 3 sets of 8 reps, for example, you would push to make the eighth rep hard enough that you would only be able to perform two more with good form.

"The final rep that you do is going to be slower than the earlier reps that you did, and is going to be hard and challenging," Samuel says. "You might have a little bit of doubt whether you can actually complete the rep with good form."

That struggle is what you're aiming to achieve. Samuel says that it's okay if your form wavers a bit near the end—but make sure that you don't push beyond your limits.

If you find yourself finishing that final rep easily, on the other hand, that doesn't mean to push to 10 or 12 reps for every set. Instead, use that as a gauge of your effort. That's a sign to use heavier weights so that you can reach the level of intensity you're aiming to achieve.

Want to know more about building real muscle? Check out the [_MH_ Ultimate Guide to Muscle-Building](https://www.menshealth.com/fitness/a64058757/muscle-building-plan/), which features the advice, exercises, and training plans you need to achieve your goals.

**Visibility**: Public

## Transcript

so just to just to bring the viewers up to speed because I think that um so one of the things we talked about when when the recording was cut off is like you know why are people interested in rpe and Reps and Reserve in the first place and it relates back to what we were just talking about where if you're making a percentage-based program you know if you say you know I want people to be doing sets of five at eighty percent uh what you have in mind is like I want people to be doing reasonably hard but non-failure sets at 80 percent but if someone has really good strength endurance those sets of five might be really easy if someone has piss poor strength endurance though you know it might be training to failure from set one and now the program's way too hard and so yeah people are interested in rpe and rir to try to to normalize the stimulus so it's similar for everyone um but you're you're raising a very good point which is like one you need to know you need to know what those those numbers are referring to like if someone says that set was an rpe 9 I had one rep in reserve the next question is is how do you know that and one of the things that I've that I've noticed just kind of observing lifters online observing lifters in the gym is I think that there's kind of a generational gap of the pre-rpe generation in the post rpe generation because you know I I know that when I got into lifting I just trained a failure a lot um and like I still do it's it's fun I like working hard and so like something like bicep curls yeah I'm never going to do bicep curls with three reps in reserve like I'm doing I'm doing it to failure or pass failure um but yeah so if you already have a lot of experience with training to failure if someone says hey do a set with two reps in reserve you say okay cool that's easy I know what that feels like but if someone if like a younger lifter who came into the sport when RP reps and Reserve were already very popular then you know maybe they don't have that much experience training to failure and if you tell them hey do a set of squats with three reps left in reserve and then you see the video and you just know as a coach there were eight reps in reserve um you know they they need like they just don't know how many reps in reserve they have and and in research we call that anchoring the scale so if you if you're telling someone hey uh you know zero reps in reserve that's like one extreme end of the scale zero reps in reserve 10 rpe the way that you anchor that scale is actually get people to train to failure so that they then know what that feels like and can then work backwards and sort out what everything else feels like and I think that with a lot of lifters like rpe and Reps and Reserve work theoretically but they don't work in practice because they've never adequately anchored that scale um and like you mentioned it it's also going to depend on the way you're doing reps and ultimately when you're writing programs this comes down to like what is the purpose the coach had for writing the exercise they did and the the particular training variables they did so you know if someone one's doing bicep curls purely just for biceps hypertrophy and they're you know taking their time squeezing everything controlling The Eccentric uh you know two reps in reserve might feel very different than someone who I guess is like training for maximal biceps power and doing reps accordingly um so yeah ultimately I think it I think it's down to the coach to be clear to the lifter or the person buying their program like ultimately what is the purpose of this exercise to like contextualize how how they're supposed to feel and what they're supposed to be getting out of it okay that that brings up the next question so if we're going staying on the strength field and not body filming but the lower the lower exercises are going to be basically just there for hypertrophy right I mean doing sets eight to ten on you know crossbody tricep extensions or something like that probably isn't going to have a whole lot of Correspondence to pushing your bench up except maybe the endurance aspect the slight muscle endurance out but it's for the triceps so down in there would it be let's just go would it be better right for the lifter to have that slower intent to where they don't use momentum to start it keep it more controlled you know Flex for a second use lighter this is where I'm going here use a lighter load right because it's going to be somewhat maybe better on the joints I'm hesitant to say anything concretely right because there's so many nuances with that but I would say it's easier on the joints because it's lighter load you're going to be able to get more range of motion take the momentum out or to just keep it heavy right let's say say for yourself the difference between maybe using a 30 pound dumbbell 25 pound dumbbell or using a 60 pound dumbbell both for the sets of six to eight you know for that hypertrophy one you're going to go a little slower both you're going to be in the same rir which one would be best a great idea can be phasic I get that but which one would be best for the person that's looking for the hypertrophy realistically I don't think it matters all that much like I think as long as like as long as the set's pretty hard you're getting pretty close to failure um you know if someone is using all of the momentum in the world for their bicep curls or whatever like ultimately they're still going to fail because their biceps gave out like that that is going to be the link that fails um but I do think that that from a practical perspective uh what you're getting at has a lot of Merit I also wouldn't make any like really strong evidentiary claims that go in slower using lighter weights is definitely safer and is definitely going to reduce injury risk but like I kind of think it will and I I'll note I also used to make the um I I would sometimes argue and push back against that because I thought that uh you know when you're really isolating a muscle you if you're like slightly more ballistic about it that might put a little bit more tendon strain on you which might be better for tendon adaptations like like that that sounds like a dangerous thing tendon strain that's going to hurt you as long as the load's manageable like you'll probably be fine and just you know just like you need particular stimuli to make your muscles adapt you need particular stimuli to make your tendons adapt as well so I would I would sometimes previously argue that like ah maybe getting a little bit more crazy with it might actually be good sometimes because that's going to give you more tendon strain better for tendon adaptations and make everything else you do a little bit safer but there was actually a meta-analysis that just came out um looking at the effect of of different types of training on Tenon adaptations and it seems like load ultimately doesn't have much of an effect on how well tendons adapt so you know that that takes away that argument in favor of maybe going heavier and uh being slightly more ballistic with single joint training so yeah now I'm I'm fully on board with yeah reduce the weight control it take your time uh and and do your single joint bodybuilding style training like accessory work in in that fashion so if you're doing it with you know more explosive intent from the beginning like you're like the heavier stuff we're talking about does that a have any change on the firing pattern you know the muscle right the firing and B if it does is it even significant I mean it probably does but then the question is like to what extent does that matter so if you really train your triceps to fire with optimal efficiency for a crossbody tricep extension is that necessarily going to make them more efficient when they're firing turn for the bench press yeah and I kind of don't think so no no for I I get what you're saying so the specificity of what's higher up in the chain the reason for doing it in the first place yeah so now if it's just hypertrophy forget all that but just to grow the tricep is that going to make any difference I don't think so no okay um and then staying on this you know RP or all the other kind of stuff where um the the wraps right the the variance in there how am I going to try to ask this because we we based it on the intensity right so as where we embassed their now if it's effort right so now the effort you're talking what you're talking about earlier kind of the metabolic demands of all that is that because in my brain I kind of see that as two different scales are those two different scales or is it kind of just one in the same or is it only two different for me because I have all the experience I know what it feels like to do this at and be on the floor after a set of 10. like are are you asking about like the general correspondence between like well just the effort right so yeah going to the old Meathead adage right like you're not training hard where it's like okay what what's that even mean you know I do think they're getting it slightly different concepts yes um you know if you're if you're training in kind of a Zen State like you're you're one of those low low uh low intensity type lifters not not intensity in terms of weight on the bar but like you're just not a super intense person in the gym um you know then then you could legitimately do a set legitimately stop it when you have two reps in reserve but then crank the music up to 11 hit an ammonia cap get kind of crazy with it and then do that exact same set but have four reps in reserve if you stopped like at the exact same number of reps um like because you were in a real sense like putting more effort into the set um so yeah I do think that like rpe will certainly scale with effort like a all else being equal a higher rpe lower rir set is going to be a set that requires more effort but if all else isn't equal and your your overall approach to that set differs um you know you could have you could have the same rpe but like very different levels of effort in a set and very different levels of performance is it um I guess what I'm trying to get at is let's say on one training day you know I'm doing say one week I do that exercise the next week I do the same exercise so the first week I'm bat crazy when I do that exercising that's at rpe eight that second week I do the same thing but not bad crazy and still the rpe eight are the recovery demands the same I think they're probably not but it's it's hard to say for sure okay um yeah I do think that if you like really amp yourself up for a workout uh it will probably be more challenging to recover from even if you leave the same number of reps in reserve per set um but I I also can't pretend like I have any research to cite yeah I know I know we're just talking here because the way that I would kind of go about that is to tell whoever it is it's it's all still phasic right so if you're gonna if you're to not do it every other week would be my you know to scale it like you go three weeks or you're going hard like that and then three weeks where it's the other way you know so that way you can control it control your control pulse you know a little bit more because what I think happens a lot with intermediate and above is maybe every other exercise might be that way than the other ones that way yeah right and then that impacts that next training week say when it's not that way it's obviously not as hard and then they're mentally racked yeah yeah and that now they need to try to find a way to get even more yeah amped up to now we're starting to deal with recovery demands of just the mental part of being that ramped up all the time where at what point does that become more of the concern too now that I just said that because now there's a difference between the person that goes you know arousal curve top yeah right that's obviously going to take more to recover from than slightly down on the arousal curve yeah you know I think I think most people aren't idiots and I think most people kind of figure that out for themselves um like I think when people go from you know just finding a program on the internet and saying I'm gonna do this to taking that first step of let's learn a little bit more about this generally the one of the very first things they'll learn is the importance of progressive overload and I think when you're introduced to that concept but your mind is still in a very naive State and you interpret that as oh Progressive overload that just means I have to do more every single week and that will get me bigger and stronger and everything's great that tends to work quite well for uh three four five six maybe 12 weeks or whatever but eventually you find yourself in that state you were just describing where you know used to be for every lift you're doing it wasn't that hard to add five more pounds add one more rep but then eventually it is yeah and you you have to cut a corner or have to get a little bit more amped up to get that extra rep or add that extra five pounds and then next week oh I have to get even more amped up or uh like cut Corners even a little bit more to add a little more weight or one more rep and I think most people have the experience of going down that road and just running themselves into the ground pretty early in their training career and even if they don't fully think through why that is I think that experience kind of sticks with them and just on an implicit level they realize okay whatever that was I can't do that again not with every exercise not all the time and so I think with like a phasic structure of training that's a good way to minimize risk so that if you if someone is pushing that way in a really intense block of training they can never dig themselves that big of a hole because that phase might only be three four five weeks and then they're gonna have time to recover um but I think even if someone isn't thinking about things in that tour in those terms if they just have a decent head on their shoulders that that first experience of just running themselves into the ground I think they take lessons from that no I would agree with that
